fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Simulation is a relation between two systems in which one system can mimic the observable behavior of the other.
From a linguistic perspective, we may say that simulation involves a correspondence of the syntax but not of the semantics of two languages or systems.

The key terms used in this definition are "mimic" and "correspondence". Where artifacts are concerned:
Mimicry cannot be said to be categorically objective, because mimicry entails purposeful imitation. Thus, whether a system is mimicking another is observer-relative. That is, we cannot speak of any purposeful intent that is intrinsic to the artifact or its behavior, but we can assign an interpretation to the behavior of a system S' that ascribes to it and some other system S the relational property "S' mimicks S". The decision to assign an interpretation is rooted in the observer's purposes and some accidental features and affordances of both systems that the observer finds relevant, similar or useful.
Correspondence is not identity—two systems need not share the same syntax—but the case where the syntax of one systems or language can be related to that of the other in an analogical and structure-preserving way.

In a formal language context, we may analyze simulation in terms of the syntactic and semantic properties of two systems. We may say that a system S' simulates another system S if there is an extensional correspondence between the syntactic terms of S' and S such that the denotations of the terms of S' is equivalent to the denotations of the terms of S. In other words, the denotational semantics of S' is equivalent to the denotational semantics of S under some interpretation of S' that relates terms in S' to terms in S.
\begin {array}{c} \forall e'{:}S'\exists e{:}S . \llbracket e' \rrbracket _{S'} = \llbracket e \rrbracket _S \\ \forall e{:}S \exists e'{:}S' . \llbracket e \rrbracket _{S} = \llbracket e' \rrbracket _{S'} \end {array}

Denotational semantics is the basis for simulation, because two corresponding systems are indiscernible under denotational semantics alone. This is due to the extensionality of such a semantics coupled with an interpretation. (If the syntactic terms are identical in both systems, then we have a tidy demonstration of two coextensive systems that are nonetheless not necessarily equivalent.) We can, however, distinguish the simulation from the real McCoy on intensional grounds, which we can do with an operational semantics. Operationally, we may say that a system S' simulates another system S if there is a correspondence between the terms of S' and S such that the evaluation of a term of S' is equivalent to the evaluation of a term in S and vice versa.
